data.description:
## Why

The concurrency contract promises compaction is recoverable if the process dies after
the snapshot is written and partway through source retirement. Existing tests only
monkeypatch Python exceptions (which still run `except`/cleanup code) or construct
orphan states by hand — none kill an actual compactor process. `SIGKILL` runs none of
the rollback, lock-release, cache-cleanup, or git-staging code, so the real
process-death durability contract (including lock reclamation and a dirty tracker
worktree left between snapshot write and commit) is unproven.

## What

- Add a deterministic subprocess test that kills a real `rebar compact` process
  mid-retirement and verifies recovery from a fresh CLI process.
- Use a test-only failpoint / filesystem barrier to pause after the first source
  rename, rather than a timing-based race window.

## Scope

- `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/` (new process-death test)
- Possibly a small test-only failpoint hook in `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py`
  (guarded so it is inert in production).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Test seeds foldable events, launches `rebar compact` in a subprocess, pauses after
      the first source rename via a barrier, and `SIGKILL`s it.
- [ ] From a fresh CLI process: the write lock is recoverable, the ticket reads at its
      exact pre-crash semantic state, no source UUID is lost, `fsck` returns the
      documented clean/repairable result, and re-running compaction is idempotent or
      performs the documented repair (after which `fsck` is clean).
- [ ] The test is deterministic (no reliance on a narrow timing window).
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.
data.title: Add real compactor process-death (SIGKILL) recovery test
